title: Data Layer Patterns: Source-Dataset-Shape Architecture
topic: Rally App SDK 2.x WsapiDataStore + ModelFactory, TanStack Query queryOptions, Relay GraphQL data layer, SWR keyed fetching, Zustand selector pattern — compared on: one source → multiple shape projections, shared fetch deduplication, typed dataset registry with linting, mutation invalidation to derived views, SDK-level introspection. Goal: inform design of a Vector datasource layer on the addressable substrate.
summary: Five data-layer patterns compared on projection, deduplication, typed registry, invalidation, and introspection — with a proposed Vector design.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Rally App SDK 2.x — WsapiDataStore + ModelFactory
  4. 4. TanStack Query — queryOptions + Query Key Factory
  5. 5. Relay — Fragment-Projection + Normalized Store
  6. 6. SWR — Keyed Fetch + Provider Cache
  7. 7. Zustand — Selector-Derived State
  8. 8. DevExtreme DataSource — Stateful Projection Layer
  9. 9. Comparison Matrix
  10. 10. Detailed Analysis — Five Dimensions
  11. 11. Vector Proposal — Source / Dataset / Shape Architecture
  12. 12. Gaps
  13. 13. Sources

1. Overview

This paper surveys five production data-layer patterns — Rally App SDK 2.x (WsapiDataStore + ModelFactory), TanStack Query (queryOptions + Query Key Factory), Relay (fragment-projection + normalized store), SWR (keyed fetch + provider cache), and Zustand (selector-derived state) — plus DevExtreme's DataSource as an additional reference. The goal is to identify which concepts transfer directly to Vector's use case: a typed dataset registry that sits on the addressable substrate (samantha._dataset.*), where sources (fetch functions) are registered separately from datasets (source + projection → typed shape), and consumers bind by dataset id, not URL.

The question driving the comparison is not "which library should we use?" but "which architectural ideas should we borrow, and what is genuinely novel about combining a typed dataset registry with an addressable substrate and lint enforcement?"

2. Key Findings

  • No existing library separates sources from datasets. Every library surveyed couples the fetch function to a single shape. Relay comes closest — its normalized store is shape-agnostic and fragments are projections — but Relay requires GraphQL on both sides. None of the REST-oriented libraries (Rally, TanStack, SWR) support "one network call, multiple shape projections" natively.
  • TanStack Query's queryOptions DataTag is the closest analogue to a typed registry. It encodes the queryFn return type into the queryKey at the TypeScript type level, enabling getQueryData to return the right type without explicit annotation. This is the registry-linking mechanism worth borrowing.
  • Rally's ModelFactory is the closest analogue to a named type registry. ModelFactory.getModel({ type: 'UserStory' }) abstracts the endpoint behind a normalized type name. Consumers never see a URL. The weakness: one model = one shape = one endpoint. No projection layer exists.
  • Relay's fragment-masking + normalized store is the gold standard for projection. Multiple fragments on the same underlying User type produce different field selections; all share one network round-trip because the compiler stitches them into a single query. Mutation to a User node propagates to every mounted fragment automatically. The cost: requires GraphQL; not applicable to REST or custom server shapes.
  • SWR and TanStack Query deduplicate at the key level, not the source level. Two useQuery hooks with identical queryKey share a single network request. But if two datasets project from the same underlying fetch with different transforms, they get two requests — there is no concept of a shared source below the key.
  • Zustand's selector pattern solves the derived-view problem within a single store. Multiple components select different slices of the same store state; a mutation writes to the store once and every selector re-evaluates. Closest to what we want for "shared source, multiple shapes" — but scoped to in-memory state, not network data.
  • Lint enforcement of query keys is nascent. TanStack Query ships an ESLint plugin with four rules; none validate that a string key is registered. The exhaustive-deps rule enforces that queryFn dependencies appear in the queryKey — the strongest static guarantee in the ecosystem. No library has "fail CI on unknown dataset id". That is genuinely new.
  • SDK-level introspection exists in TanStack and Relay, not in the others. queryClient.getQueryCache().getAll() lists every registered query instance at runtime. Relay's Store exposes get(dataID), getType(), and invalidateRecord(). Neither exposes a static registry — you can only inspect what is currently mounted. A Vector dataset registry backed by the addressable substrate would be statically enumerable at build time and at runtime, which is novel.
  • DevExtreme's DataSource = DataSource (state) + Store (access) split is the cleanest prior art for "one store, multiple projections." Multiple DataSource instances wrapping one Store each maintain independent sort/filter/group state. This is architecturally close to the proposed Vector "one source, multiple datasets" model.

3. Rally App SDK 2.x — WsapiDataStore + ModelFactory

Architecture

Rally's data layer is built on ExtJS (Sencha) and exposes two key abstractions: Rally.data.ModelFactory (type registry) and Rally.data.wsapi.Store (data fetcher bound to a model). The ModelFactory maintains an internal types hash mapping normalized type strings ('userstory', 'defect', 'portfolioitem/feature') to factory class references. Factories are registered with ModelFactory.registerType(typeName, factory) or registerTypes([names], factory). Consumers call ModelFactory.getModel({ type: 'UserStory', success, failure }) — the factory resolves asynchronously and returns the model definition including field list and WSAPI endpoint.

The WsapiDataStore accepts model: 'UserStory' (string) and delegates hydration to ModelFactory.getModel() internally. Consumers configure the store with fetch (field list), filters, sorters, and context (workspace/project scope). Data comes back via callbacks (load({ callback: fn })) or Deft.Deferred promises. The store fires standard ExtJS load events.

What it gets right

  • Consumers bind by model name, not URL. Renaming an endpoint is a one-line factory change.
  • Type normalization ('User Story' and 'userstory' both resolve to the same model) prevents string-typo bugs at registration time, not call time.
  • The factory pattern enables lazy model loading — the schema is fetched from the server on first use.

What it bakes in too early

  • One model = one shape = one endpoint. There is no projection layer; if a board and a tree both need UserStory data, they each instantiate a separate store and issue two requests, even if the field lists overlap.
  • No shared-source deduplication between stores. Two store instances on the same model type do not share a fetch.
  • No static lint enforcement. String typos in type: 'UsreStory' blow up at runtime.
  • Introspection is limited: ModelFactory.getFactory(type) checks if a type is registered, but there is no enumerable registry of all types. Custom-app authors cannot ask "what models exist?" without an external documentation source.

4. TanStack Query — queryOptions + Query Key Factory

Architecture

TanStack Query v5 introduced queryOptions() — a lightweight runtime no-op that associates TypeScript type information from the queryFn with the queryKey via a mechanism called the DataTag (a TypeScript symbol). The DataTag encodes [dataTagSymbol]: ReturnType<QueryFn> into the key's type, making queryClient.getQueryData(key) return the inferred type without explicit annotation.

The community Query Key Factory pattern (lukemorales/query-key-factory) extends this with createQueryKeys and mergeQueryKeys to build a typed object of query definitions grouped by domain (userQueries.detail(id) returns a queryOptions-wrapped config).

Deduplication is key-based: two useQuery hooks with the same queryKey array share one cache entry and one network request. The QueryCache.getAll() method lists all query instances at runtime. The QueryObserver pattern tracks mounted subscribers per query.

What it gets right

  • The DataTag pattern is the cleanest typed registry link in the ecosystem — the key carries the return type and getQueryData / setQueryData infer from it.
  • The select option on useQuery is a native projection layer: useQuery({ ...profileQueryOptions, select: (p) => p.name }) returns a string, not a Profile. Different components can project different shapes from the same cache entry without a second network request.
  • Key-level deduplication is automatic and transparent. Any two hooks with matching keys share data.
  • Runtime introspection via getQueryCache().getAll() + React Query DevTools.

What it bakes in too early

  • Deduplication is at the key level, not the source level. If two datasets need the same underlying fetch but different top-level keys (e.g. ['org_topology', 'graph'] and ['org_topology', 'tree']), they issue two requests. There is no concept of a shared fetch function below the key.
  • The select projection works only within one useQuery call — it does not create a named, reusable, statically-typed projection that other components can bind to by id.
  • ESLint plugin rules (exhaustive-deps, no-deprecated-options, prefer-query-object-syntax, stable-query-client) do not validate registry membership. String typos in queryKey arrays are not caught statically.
  • No build-time enumeration of registered queries — only runtime introspection of mounted instances.

5. Relay — Fragment-Projection + Normalized Store

Architecture

Relay's data layer is the most sophisticated in the survey. The Store is a normalized in-memory cache keyed by data ID (__id). All fetched data is merged into the store by type and ID — there is never more than one copy of a given record. The Environment wraps the network layer and the store, managing query execution, caching, and garbage collection.

Fragments are the projection mechanism. Each React component declares its data dependencies as a GraphQL fragment; the Relay compiler stitches all fragments in a tree into a single optimized query. Multiple fragments on the same underlying type produce different field selections — all share one network round-trip. useFragment(fragmentDef, ref) subscribes a component to exactly the fields it declared; data masking prevents a parent from accidentally reading a child's fields.

Mutations propagate through the store: when a mutation response is received, Relay finds the matching record by ID, updates it, and re-renders only the components whose subscribed fragment fields changed. invalidateRecord(dataID) marks a record stale for targeted refetch. invalidateStore() marks everything stale.

What it gets right

  • One source → multiple shape projections is the core design principle. This is the best implementation of the concept in the survey.
  • Mutation propagation to all derived views sharing the same underlying record is automatic and O(1) — no explicit invalidation keys needed.
  • The compiler enforces type safety at build time: every fragment generates and types. Typos in field names fail the compiler, not the runtime.
  • Runtime store introspection via RecordProxy.getType(), getDataID(), getLinkedRecord() — full traversal of the normalized graph.

What it bakes in too early

  • Requires GraphQL on the server. REST APIs, custom server shapes (like Vector's RelationsPayload or OrgNode[]), and Next.js route handlers cannot participate.
  • Fragment colocation is component-scoped — you cannot define a named, reusable dataset projection that multiple unrelated components bind to by id. The binding is via fragment spread in JSX, not a string registry lookup.
  • No addressable substrate — there is no concept of a named element in a page that corresponds to a dataset. The Relay Environment is global; components are not named or introspectable by the SDK consumer.

6. SWR — Keyed Fetch + Provider Cache

Architecture

SWR (stale-while-revalidate) uses a string key + fetcher function model. The cache is a Map-like provider (configurable via SWRConfig). Key-level deduplication within a dedupingInterval (default 2 s) ensures one network request per key regardless of how many components call useSWR(key, fetcher). Nested SWRConfig providers scope the cache per subtree.

Invalidation is via mutate(key) (refetch) or mutate(key, data) (optimistic). The predicate form mutate(key => key.startsWith('user/'), ...) invalidates a key pattern.

What it gets right

  • Minimal API. One hook, one key, one fetcher. Low ceremony for simple use cases.
  • Key-level deduplication is robust and well-understood.
  • Provider model allows per-subtree cache isolation — useful for multi-tenant or multi-workspace views.

What it bakes in too early

  • No typed registry. Keys are plain strings; no DataTag mechanism; useSWR<Profile>(key, fetcher) requires manual type annotation. Typos in keys fail silently.
  • No projection layer. One key = one shape. No select equivalent.
  • No source-level deduplication. Two keys with the same underlying fetcher issue two requests.
  • No build-time or static introspection. Only runtime cache inspection.

7. Zustand — Selector-Derived State

Architecture

Zustand is a minimalist state store. Consumers subscribe via selectors: const name = useStore(state => state.user.name). The selector function runs on every state update; Zustand compares the previous and next selected value with Object.is() (or a custom equality function) and re-renders only if they differ. Multiple components selecting different slices from the same store decouple their re-render cycles.

Static selectors defined outside components are a best practice; useShallow wraps selectors that return objects/arrays to do shallow comparison. createWithEqualityFn allows custom equality for derived objects.

What it gets right

  • The selector pattern is the cleanest model for "one source, multiple derived shapes" — within in-memory state. A mutation writes to the store once; every selector re-evaluates immediately without explicit invalidation.
  • Re-render isolation: components subscribing to disjoint slices do not re-render each other.
  • Static selectors are composable and reusable across components without creating a new network concept.

What it bakes in too early

  • In-memory only. Zustand does not fetch data; it stores data already in memory. Combining it with a fetch layer (SWR, TanStack, custom) requires an adapter — the fetch result is written into the store, then selectors project from it. This is exactly the source/dataset split — but without library support, it's boilerplate.
  • No typed registry. Selectors are ad-hoc functions; there is no named, enumerable registry of projections.
  • No lint enforcement. Selector typos fail at runtime.

8. DevExtreme DataSource — Stateful Projection Layer

Architecture

DevExtreme separates the access layer (Store: ArrayStore, ODataStore, CustomStore) from the projection/state layer (DataSource). Multiple DataSource instances can wrap the same Store, each maintaining independent sort, filter, group, select, and map configurations. DataSource applies transformations in a defined pipeline: filter → sort → select → group → page → map → postProcess. The push(changes) method notifies bound DataSource instances of mutations, optionally triggering a full transformation re-run (reshapeOnPush).

What it gets right

  • The Store/DataSource split is the closest prior art to the proposed Vector source/dataset split. One Store → many DataSources = one fetch → many projections.
  • The select property on DataSource is a first-class projection primitive, not an afterthought.
  • The push + reshapeOnPush mechanism is a clean invalidation model: mutation hits the store, all bound DataSources re-apply their projections.

What it bakes in too early

  • No typed registry. DataSources are instantiated imperatively; there is no enumerable registry of named projections.
  • Not designed for React hooks — the API is class-based and imperative; integrating with React requires wrapper hooks.
  • No lint enforcement or addressable substrate.

9. Comparison Matrix

Dimension Rally 2.x TanStack Query Relay SWR Zustand DevExtreme
Named type registry Yes (ModelFactory) Partial (queryOptions DataTag) Yes (GraphQL schema) No No No
One source → multiple shapes No Partial (select per hook) Yes (fragments) No Yes (selectors, in-memory) Yes (Store + DataSource)
Shared fetch deduplication No (per-store) Yes (per queryKey) Yes (compiler batching) Yes (per key, 2 s window) N/A (in-memory) No (per DataSource)
Typed registry link String type → factory DataTag symbol on queryKey Compiler-generated / types Manual annotation Selector return type No
Mutation → derived view propagation No (re-load per store) Manual invalidateQueries() Automatic (normalized by ID) mutate(key) + key pattern Automatic (selector re-eval) push() + reshapeOnPush
Lint enforcement of registry None exhaustive-deps rule only Compiler (GraphQL AST) None None None
Runtime introspection getFactory(type) getQueryCache().getAll() Store.get(id), getType() Cache.keys() getState() DataSource.items()
Build-time enumerable registry No No Yes (schema) No No No
Addressable substrate No No No No No No

10. Detailed Analysis — Five Dimensions

10.1 One source → multiple shape projections

Only Relay and DevExtreme genuinely solve this at the library level. Relay's compiler stitches fragments into one query; DevExtreme's DataSource wraps one Store with independent transformations. TanStack Query's select option provides per-hook projection but does not deduplicate at the source level — two useQuery calls with different keys but the same underlying fetch still issue two requests.

The Zustand model is instructive even though it's in-memory: write once to the source, read many times with selectors. If the fetch result is written into a Zustand slice and selectors project from it, you get source-level deduplication at the cost of boilerplate. This is the "source layer separate from dataset layer" pattern, implemented manually.

10.2 Shared fetch deduplication across components

TanStack Query and SWR deduplicate at the key level — any number of mounted useQuery hooks with the same key share one in-flight request and one cache entry. This is robust and well-tested. The limit is that deduplication requires identical keys: if org_topology_graph and org_topology_tree are different keys but share the same underlying topologyApi.tree() call, they each issue a separate fetch.

Source-level deduplication (multiple datasets sharing one fetch) requires explicit architecture: a shared source layer that is separate from the dataset projection layer. None of the surveyed libraries provide this. DevExtreme comes closest conceptually but its Store does not cache the network response; each DataSource triggers its own fetch.

10.3 Typed dataset registry with linting / static analysis

TanStack Query's DataTag mechanism is the best type-safety story for REST APIs. The DataTag symbol encodes the return type into the queryKey's TypeScript type, making getQueryData and setQueryData type-aware without manual annotation. The Query Key Factory pattern (createQueryKeys) adds structure to key hierarchies but remains runtime-only — no static enumeration.

Relay's GraphQL compiler is the gold standard: every fragment reference generates typed and interfaces; field typos fail the compiler, not the runtime; the schema is a statically-analyzable artifact. This level of enforcement is not achievable with REST without a custom build step.

TanStack Query's ESLint plugin ships four rules; none validate registry membership. The exhaustive-deps rule ensures queryFn closure dependencies appear in the queryKey — the strongest static guarantee available without a custom rule. No library has a "fail CI on unknown dataset id" rule. That pattern is genuinely novel and is exactly what Vector's lint:dataset-references would implement.

10.4 Mutation invalidation propagation to derived views

Relay's normalized store is automatic: a mutation that updates a User node propagates to every fragment reading that node, regardless of which component declared the fragment. This is the most elegant invalidation model — no explicit keys, no manual invalidateQueries calls.

TanStack Query requires explicit queryClient.invalidateQueries(['userStories']) after a mutation. The key pattern form (['userStories'] invalidates all queries whose key starts with that prefix) is powerful but requires the developer to maintain the key hierarchy mentally.

SWR's mutate(keyPredicate) is equivalent but more primitive — no key hierarchy, pure string matching. DevExtreme's store.push(changes) + reshapeOnPush is declarative: push a change, all bound DataSources re-apply their projections. Zustand's selector model is automatic within in-memory state.

For Vector's use case — where mutations happen in Go handlers and the frontend learns about them via NOTIFY events — the TanStack / SWR pattern of key-based invalidation on event receipt is the practical path. Source-level invalidation (invalidate the source, all datasets re-project) would be more elegant but requires the source layer to be explicit.

10.5 SDK-level introspection

Only TanStack Query and Relay provide meaningful runtime introspection. queryClient.getQueryCache().getAll() returns all query instances with their keys, state, observers, and metadata — the basis for React Query DevTools. Relay's Store traversal (get(dataID), getType(), getLinkedRecord()) allows programmatic inspection of the normalized graph.

No library supports build-time enumeration of all registered datasets/queries — you can only inspect what is currently mounted. A Vector dataset registry backed by the addressable substrate would be build-time enumerable (the registry module is statically analyzable), addressable at runtime (every dataset has a samantha._dataset.* address), and introspectable via the Samantha SDK — which is genuinely novel in this space.

11. Vector Proposal — Source / Dataset / Shape Architecture

The core idea, grounded in prior art

The proposed Vector data layer borrows from four places:

  1. ModelFactory (Rally) — named type registry; consumers bind by id, not URL.
  2. DataTag (TanStack Query) — the queryKey carries the return type; typed registry links key → shape.
  3. Store/DataSource split (DevExtreme) — one access object, multiple projection objects.
  4. Selector model (Zustand) — write once to source, project many times.

What none of these provide, and what Vector adds:

  • Source-level deduplication — multiple datasets backed by the same source share one in-flight fetch. If org_topology_graph and org_topology_tree share the source id "topology", only one topologyApi.tree() call is made per mount cycle.
  • Addressable substrate integration — datasets register as samantha._dataset.org_topology; the help-icon, RBAC, and lint systems apply automatically, the same as for panels, tables, and trees.
  • Build-time lint enforcement — lint:dataset-references walks the call graph, finds all useDataset("id") calls, and fails on unregistered ids. No existing library does this.

Proposed module structure

app/lib/
  datasource.ts          // registerSource, registerDataset, useDataset
  datasets/
    work_items_relations.ts  // source: fetch /api/v2/work-items/relations
    org_topology.ts          // source: topologyApi.tree(); datasets: graph + tree
    portfolio_layers.ts      // future

API surface (minimal Pass A)

// datasource.ts
registerSource(id: string, fetch: () => Promise<unknown>): void

registerDataset<S, T>(opts: {
  id: string
  source: string               // source id (shared fetch)
  shape: 'graph' | 'tree' | 'page'
  project: (raw: S) => T      // adapter: raw source data → typed shape
}): void

function useDataset<T>(id: string): {
  data: T | null
  loading: boolean
  error: Error | null
  refetch: () => Promise<void>
}

Type safety — borrowing TanStack's DataTag idea

At registration time, the dataset id is tagged with its output type via a TypeScript symbol on the registry object. useDataset("org_topology") returns RelationsPayload | null without explicit annotation, because the registry knows what "org_topology" produces.

// The registry maps id → { shape: T } via a typed lookup table
type DatasetRegistry = {
  org_topology: RelationsPayload
  org_topology_tree: OrgTreePayload
  work_items_relations: RelationsPayload
}

// useDataset infers T from the registry
function useDataset<K extends keyof DatasetRegistry>(id: K): {
  data: DatasetRegistry[K] | null
  ...
}

Source-level deduplication

The source layer maintains a Map<sourceId, Promise<unknown>>. When two datasets backed by the same source are mounted simultaneously, the second one receives the same promise, not a new fetch. This is source-level deduplication — not available in TanStack Query or SWR without identical keys.

Invalidation model

Sources respond to domain events (topology:changed, work_items:changed) via the existing event bus. When an event fires, the source re-fetches and notifies all mounted datasets backed by it. Datasets re-project from the new raw data and notify their useDataset hooks. No explicit key management needed by consumers.

Lint rule: lint:dataset-references

A Python AST walker (mirroring lint:addressables and lint:role-literals) parses all .ts / .tsx files for useDataset("...") and dataset="..." attribute strings, cross-references against the registered dataset ids in app/lib/datasets/index.ts, and fails on unknown ids. An exemption ledger at dev/registries/datasets.json allows temporary bypasses with a mandatory expiry date.

What is genuinely novel

ConceptPrior artWhat Vector adds
Named registry Rally ModelFactory, TanStack queryOptions Registry is addressable substrate — every dataset has a samantha.* address
Typed projection TanStack select, Relay fragments Named reusable projection bound by id, not inline per-hook
Source deduplication None (key-level only in TanStack/SWR) Source-level: multiple datasets share one in-flight fetch
Lint enforcement TanStack exhaustive-deps, Relay compiler CI fails on unregistered dataset id string — no library does this for REST
SDK introspection TanStack getQueryCache(), Relay Store.get() Static enumerable at build time via registry module; runtime via samantha._dataset.*

12. Gaps

  • The TanStack Query v4 documentation URLs returned 404 during this research; v5 documentation was used as the primary source for queryOptions and query-key-factory. Verify that DataTag behaviour is the same in v4 vs v5 before referencing in implementation.
  • Relay's introspection API surface for runtime store traversal was not fully documented in the sources fetched — the RecordProxy API was confirmed but the full environment.check() / garbage-collection lifecycle was not explored.
  • Jotai and Recoil (atom-graph state libraries) were not surveyed; they may have relevant patterns for derived-atom projection that sit between Zustand (single store) and Relay (normalized graph).
  • The lukemorales/query-key-factory npm package documentation was unavailable during the crawl (404); findings on createQueryKeys / mergeQueryKeys are based on secondary sources.
  • No performance benchmarks were found comparing source-level vs key-level deduplication under concurrent mount scenarios — this is relevant for Vector's 50k+ node graphs where the topology fetch may be expensive.
  • The proposed lint:dataset-references rule assumes all dataset ids are string literals at the call site. Dynamic ids (useDataset(dynamicId)) would bypass the rule; this needs an explicit policy (ban dynamic ids, or require a type assertion).
